content:
# Acts Chapter 23 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, PDF pages 254-255 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_023_page_001.jpg, ACT_023_page_002.jpg

---

**1** Paul looked the Sanhedrin straight in the eye and said, "Brothers, I have lived my life before God with a completely clear conscience to this very day."

**2** At that, the high priest Ananias ordered those standing near Paul to strike him on the mouth.

**3** Paul fired back, "God is going to strike *you*, you whitewashed wall! You sit there judging me by the law, and then you break the law yourself by ordering me hit?"

**4** The people standing nearby said, "How dare you insult God's high priest!"

**5** Paul replied, "I did not realize he was the high priest, brothers. For it is written: 'You must not speak evil of a ruler of your people.'"

**6** Now Paul knew that part of the council were Sadducees and part were Pharisees, so he called out in the assembly, "Brothers, I am a Pharisee, the son of a Pharisee! I am on trial because of the hope of the resurrection of the dead!"

**7** The moment he said this, an argument erupted between the Pharisees and the Sadducees, and the assembly split down the middle.

**8** For the Sadducees say there is no resurrection, no angels, and no spirits, but the Pharisees affirm all of these.

**9** A huge uproar broke out. Some of the scribes on the Pharisee side jumped up and argued fiercely: "We find nothing wrong with this man! What if a spirit or an angel really did speak to him?"

**10** The dispute turned so violent that the commander was afraid they would tear Paul apart. He ordered the soldiers to go down and pull him out of the crowd by force and bring him back to the barracks.

**11** That night, the Lord stood beside Paul and said, "Take courage! Just as you have testified about me in Jerusalem, so you must also testify in Rome."

**12** When daylight came, some of the Jews formed a conspiracy. They bound themselves with a solemn oath, vowing neither to eat nor drink until they had killed Paul.

**13** More than forty men were part of this plot.

**14** They went to the chief priests and the elders and said, "We have bound ourselves under a sacred oath to taste nothing until we have killed Paul.

**15** So here is what you do: have the Sanhedrin send a request to the commander to bring Paul down to you, as if you were going to examine his case more thoroughly. We will be ready to kill him before he even gets there."

**16** But Paul's nephew -- his sister's son -- heard about the ambush. He went to the barracks and told Paul.

**17** Paul called one of the centurions over and said, "Take this young man to the commander. He has something to report to him."

**18** So the centurion brought him to the commander and said, "The prisoner Paul called me over and asked me to bring this young man to you. He has something to tell you."

**19** The commander took the young man by the hand, drew him aside privately, and asked, "What is it you have to report?"

**20** He said, "The Jews have agreed to ask you to bring Paul down to the Sanhedrin tomorrow, as if they intended to investigate his case more carefully.

**21** Do not let them persuade you! More than forty of them are lying in wait for him. They have bound themselves with an oath not to eat or drink until they have killed him. They are ready now, just waiting for your agreement."

**22** The commander dismissed the young man with a warning: "Tell no one that you reported this to me."

**23** Then he summoned two of his centurions and ordered, "Get two hundred soldiers ready to march to Caesarea, along with seventy cavalry and two hundred spearmen. Have them ready to leave at nine tonight.

**24** Also provide mounts for Paul to ride so he can be brought safely to Governor Felix."

**25** And he wrote a letter that went something like this:

**26** "Claudius Lysias, to His Excellency Governor Felix: Greetings.

**27** This man was seized by the Jews and was about to be killed by them. I arrived with my soldiers and rescued him, having learned that he is a Roman citizen.

**28** Wanting to understand what charge they were bringing against him, I brought him down to their Sanhedrin.

**29** I found that the accusations involved disputes about their own law, but there was no charge deserving death or imprisonment.

**30** When I was informed of a plot against the man, I sent him to you at once. I have also ordered his accusers to present their case against him before you."

**31** So the soldiers carried out their orders. They took Paul during the night and brought him as far as Antipatris.

**32** The next day, they let the cavalry continue on with him while the foot soldiers returned to the barracks.

**33** When the cavalry arrived in Caesarea, they delivered the letter to the governor and presented Paul to him.

**34** The governor read the letter and asked what province Paul was from. When he learned he was from Cilicia,

**35** he said, "I will hear your case in full once your accusers arrive." Then he ordered Paul held under guard in Herod's palace.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 3 -- "Whitewashed wall":** This is one of the sharpest insults in the New Testament. A whitewashed wall looks clean on the outside but is structurally rotten underneath. Paul is calling Ananias a religious fraud -- beautiful exterior, corrupt interior. The Greek (toiche kekoniamene) carries visceral contempt. I rendered it directly because the metaphor still lands in English, but the force should be understood: this is not polite disagreement. Paul is publicly calling the highest religious authority in Judaism a hypocrite to his face. Compare Jesus's "whitewashed tombs" in Matthew 23:27 -- the same devastating image from the same prophetic tradition.

**Verse 5 -- "I did not realize he was the high priest":** This line has generated centuries of debate. Was Paul being sarcastic (implying Ananias did not act like a high priest)? Was his eyesight too poor to recognize him? Had Ananias not worn his vestments? The Sinaiticus text gives no special emphasis either way. I rendered it straightforwardly and let the ambiguity stand, because the text itself does not resolve it.

**Verse 6 -- Paul's tactical move:** Paul's declaration "I am a Pharisee" is a brilliant rhetorical maneuver. He reframes his trial from "Paul the troublemaker" to a theological dispute between Pharisees and Sadducees about resurrection -- which was the deepest fault line in Second Temple Judaism. The Sinaiticus reading includes "son of Pharisees" (huios Pharisaiou), emphasizing hereditary identity. This is not dishonesty; Paul genuinely believed in resurrection. But it is strategic.

**Verse 11 -- The Lord appears at night:** This is one of the most significant moments in Acts. Amid assassination plots and political chaos, the Lord appears to Paul personally with two words that anchor everything: "Take courage" (tharsei). The Greek is a single imperative -- bold, direct, intimate. Then comes the prophetic commission: Rome. Everything that follows in Acts -- shipwreck, snake, chains -- is framed by this nighttime promise.

**Verses 12-15 -- The oath of forty men:** The conspirators bound themselves with an anathema -- a self-curse. They literally called down God's judgment on themselves if they ate or drank before killing Paul. This was not casual hatred; it was religiously sanctioned murder. The irony is devastating: men invoking God's name to kill a man God had just personally commissioned.

**Verse 16 -- Paul's nephew:** This unnamed young man is one of the quiet heroes of the New Testament. Without his courage, the story of Christianity might have ended in a Jerusalem alley. Luke gives us no backstory -- just a boy who heard something and had the nerve to act. I love that the commander "took him by the hand" (verse 19) -- a gesture of reassurance to a frightened young informant.

**Verses 25-30 -- Lysias's letter:** Luke gives us the text of the commander's letter, and it contains a revealing spin: Lysias claims he rescued Paul because he learned Paul was a Roman citizen (verse 27). In reality, he only discovered Paul's citizenship after he had already arrested him and was about to have him flogged (22:25-29). This is a military officer covering his tracks in an official report. Luke includes this detail with quiet irony -- the reader knows the truth.

**Verse 23 -- The military escort:** Two hundred infantry, seventy cavalry, two hundred spearmen -- nearly five hundred soldiers to escort one man. This massive force reveals how seriously the Romans took the threat, and how volatile Jerusalem had become. It also shows, from a narrative perspective, the invisible hand of God fulfilling the promise of verse 11: Paul will reach Rome, and God will move an empire's military apparatus to ensure it.

-- PNEUMA
